title: Thermodynamic Approach to Momentum Transport in Dense Fluids
authors: Christopher Devik Fjeldstad, Jonas Bueie, Astrid S. de Wijn
abstract: We present a new framework for extending Chapman-Enskog theory beyond the hard-sphere fluid model. Rather than relying on effective hard sphere diameters, the approach makes use of on an exchange function which can be related to the thermodynamic properties of the system. We show that two existing extensions, including modified Enskog theory (MET), fit into this new framework. Based on our approach, we propose an alternative to MET that takes into account the potential interaction energy associated with the inter-particle interactions in the fluid. The proposed expression is applied to predicting the shear viscosity of several different simulated fluid models across a wide set of densities $0.05 \leq ρ^* \leq 0.8$ and temperatures $1.5 \leq T^* \leq 4.0$ in Lennard-Jones units. The fluid models considered include both the Weeks-Chandler-Anderson (WCA) fluid and the Lennard-Jones (LJ) fluid. At low and intermediate density, here taken to be $ρ^* \leq 0.3$, we report mean relative prediction errors between $2\%$ and $4\%$ for both these. Across all densities considered, the largest mean relative errors reported are $4.4\%$ and $8.1\%$ for the WCA fluid and LJ fluid respectively. We also investigate other interaction models, including a diatomic molecular model, in order to better understand the limitations of our approach.
summary: This work introduces an advanced theoretical framework to calculate fluid shear viscosity by replacing reliance on effective hard-sphere diameters with a function derived from the system's excess internal energy. By applying this method to LJ and WCA fluids, the authors demonstrate high predictive accuracy across wide density ranges, significantly advancing classical transport theory.
main_problem: To develop a general theoretical framework for calculating transport coefficients, specifically shear viscosity, of dense fluids that extends beyond the limitations of existing theories like Chapman-Enskog theory.
main_result: The proposed thermodynamic approach using an exchange function ($\hat{g}$) accurately predicts the shear viscosity of various fluid models (WCA, LJ) with low mean relative errors (2%-4%) at low to intermediate densities.
method: The authors reformulate transport equations by relating the necessary correction term ($X_{	ext{res}}$) in an equation of state to the excess internal energy ($ eta u_{	ext{ex}}$), thus bypassing reliance on effective hard-sphere diameters.
model_or_system: The study investigates dense fluids modeled using Lennard-Jones (LJ) and Weeks-Chandler-Anderson (WCA) potentials, as well as diatomic molecules with harmonic bond interactions. Simulations are performed across a wide range of densities ($ho^*$) and temperatures ($T^*$).
key_observables: Shear viscosity ($\eta$), Compressibility factor ($Z$), Mean relative prediction error.
important_parameters: Density range: $0.05 \le ho^* \le 0.8$; Temperature range: $1.5 \le T^* \le 4.0$. Low/intermediate density regime defined as $ho^* \le 0.3$.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the necessary correction term ($X_{	ext{res}}$) can be accurately represented by a function of the excess internal energy ($ eta u_{	ext{ex}}$). Limitations exist at high densities or when inelastic collisions are dominant.
figures_summary: Figures compare predicted vs. simulated shear viscosity for Mie fluids, showing good agreement at low density; error plots show increasing errors above $ho^* \approx 0.35$.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the general framework using an exchange function ($\hat{g}$), shows how existing theories (MET) fit into it, proposes $X_{	ext{res}} =  eta u_{	ext{ex}}$, and then applies this to test predictions against simulations for various fluid models.
why_interesting: While focused on classical fluids, the rigorous development of generalized transport equations based on fundamental thermodynamic potentials ($ eta u_{	ext{ex}}$) provides a powerful template for developing similar non-equilibrium theories in quantum many-body systems where simple kinetic approximations fail.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The geometry of diatomic molecules as modeled in this paper. The intra-molecular bond is modeled using a har- monic spring. Inter-particle interactions are modeled accord- ing to the Lennard-Jones potential.

FIG. 3. Rescaled shear viscosity from simulations and pre- dictions for (a) LJ ﬂuids and (b) Mie-(10, 5) ﬂuids, using Xres = βuex. Each temperature is assigned a symbol and as- sociated dash style as denoted in the legend. For comparison in Fig. (a) the light thin gray lines give the MET prediction with dash styles given by the corresponding temperature. The shear viscosity is rescaled using the Enskog prediction for a hard-sphere ﬂuid with diameter σHS = σ and mass m = 1.

FIG. 2. Rescaled shear viscosity from simulations and predic- tions for (a) WCA ﬂuids, (b) cut and shifted Mie-(10, 5) ﬂuids and (c) cut and shifted Mie-(8, 4) ﬂuids, using Xres = βuex. Each temperature is assigned a symbol and corresponding dash style as denoted in the legend. The shear viscosity is rescaled using the Enskog prediction for a hard-sphere ﬂuid with diameter σHS = σ and mass m = 1.

FIG. 4. The relative prediction error δmean T averaged over tem- perature T ∗as a function of number density ρ∗. The errors are generally small when ρ∗≲0.3. Above this density par- ticle cores are expected to continuously overlap, three-body correlations become signiﬁcant, and the magnitude of the as- sociated prediction error increases.

FIG. 5. Rescaled shear viscosity from simulations and pre- dictions for diatomic LJ ﬂuids with spring constant k∗= 100 (a) and spring constant k∗= 1000 (b). Each temperature is assigned a symbol and associated dash style as denoted in the legend. The prediction lines are calculated using either Xres = βuex (thick lines), or Xres = Cβuex (thin lines). In the latter case, C is treated as a ﬁt parameters. The shear viscos- ity is rescaled using the Enskog prediction for a hard-sphere ﬂuid with diameter σHS = (59/32)1/3σ and mass m = 2.
